ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tandoori murgh salad. Closest defensible match is 'chicken salad' (FOODON_03302051); tandoori preparation and Indian spice profile are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for the tandoori chicken component of this mixed salad dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for tandoori murgh salad. A0EJW (cooked chicken meat) is used as the primary protein anchor. The salad and dressing components are not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tandoori murgh salad or sufficiently close prepared dish equivalent.

main_ingredients: tandoori spices (turmeric, cumin, coriander, chili, ginger, garlic)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Tandoori Murgh Salad is approximately 250 g, comprising a bed of fresh lettuce topped with grilled tandoori chicken pieces and tossed with house dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Murgh Salad — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (G00010924)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010924 for Tandoori Murgh Salad. All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical record. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 16% of total fat (7.13 g/100 g) based on typical tandoori chicken and oil-based dressing composition, as the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed salad with grilled chicken and oil-based dressing
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tandoori Murgh Salad. GI estimated at 32 (low) based on analogous mixed green salad with protein and oil-based dressing entries from the University of Sydney GI Database. The very low carbohydrate content (5.9 g/100 g) and high fat content (44.54 g/100 g) strongly suppress postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (32) × carbohydrate per serving (14.75 g for 250 g serving) / 100 = 4.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100 g — well above the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold; potassium at 64 mg/100 g provides negligible offset
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request dressing on the side and reduced salt in the marinade, and do not add any additional sodium-containing condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.9 g/100 g) and low GI (estimated 32) are favourable, but very high fat (44.54 g/100 g) raises insulin resistance risk with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Occasional consumption is acceptable for type 2 diabetics given the low carbohydrate load and low estimated GI, but the very high fat content from the dressing should be moderated by requesting dressing on the side and pairing with high-fibre vegetables or legumes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high total fat (44.54 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~7.13 g/100 g, combined with very high sodium (810 mg/100 g), raises LDL and blood pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to the combination of high saturated fat from the oil-based dressing and very high sodium; if consumed, request dressing on the side and choose a smaller portion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100 g worsens tissue swelling; saturated fat ~7.13 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g pro-inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should request reduced dressing to lower saturated fat and sodium; tandoori spices (turmeric, ginger) in the marinade provide anti-inflammatory benefit but do not offset the high sodium and saturated fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Murgh Salad is an Indian-origin restaurant dish popular in Singapore, consisting of boneless chicken marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture (tandoori masala with turmeric, cumin, coriander, chili, ginger, and garlic) and cooked in a tandoor clay oven, then served on a bed of fresh lettuce tossed with an oil-based house dressing. The dish is classified as a Chef Special and is served as a main-course salad at lunch or dinner. The chicken is characteristically smoky, slightly charred on the exterior, and tender inside, with a vibrant red-orange colour from the tandoori spice blend. The salad base provides a cool, crisp contrast to the warm, spiced chicken. Nutritionally, the dish is very high in fat (44.54 g/100 g) primarily from the oil-based dressing, low in carbohydrates (5.9 g/100 g), and very high in sodium (810 mg/100 g). Protein content is low at 1.32 g/100 g per the HPB record, which reflects the dressing-heavy composition of the dish as recorded. The estimated glycemic index is low (~32) due to the minimal carbohydrate and high fat content. The dish is not suitable for hypertension or cardiovascular disease patients due to very high sodium and fat, and should be consumed with caution by type 2 diabetics due to the high fat load. Tandoori spices including turmeric and ginger provide mild anti-inflammatory properties. The dish is halal-suitable and gluten-free when prepared with standard tandoori marinade.

ingredient_synonyms.tandoori: clay-oven grilled with spiced yogurt marinade
ingredient_synonyms.tandoori masala: spice blend of turmeric, cumin, coriander, chili, ginger, garlic

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database record G00010924 with confidence 0.95. All core macros and available micronutrients are taken from this approved primary source. Saturated fat is estimated from typical composition of tandoori chicken and oil-based dressing as the HPB record does not separately report it; this is the only adapted field.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (7.13 g/100 g) is estimated at approximately 16% of total fat based on typical fatty acid profile of tandoori chicken (predominantly MUFA from oil dressing) and is not directly reported in the HPB source record. This is the only field not directly sourced from HPB G00010924. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and full vitamin/mineral panel for this prepared dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010924 as of 2026-04-16. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is a confirmed zero from the approved source — the dish as recorded contains negligible fibre from the lettuce base, likely due to the dressing-heavy composition and the specific preparation recorded. 4. Food group corrected from 'Vegetables' to 'Meat and alternatives' as the dish is a chicken-based prepared dish; the original input classification as a vegetable dish is inconsistent with the boneless tandoori chicken as the primary protein component. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (low, ~32) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed green salad with protein and oil-based dressing; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. GL per 250 g serving = 32 × 14.75 / 100 = 4.7. 6. Protein at 1.32 g/100 g is unusually low for a chicken-based dish and likely reflects the dressing-dominant composition of the HPB-recorded formulation; this value is preserved as reported by the approved source.
